turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Is not Polemarchus your heir?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: To be sure and went away laughing to the sacrifices.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Tell me then, O thou heir of the argument, what did Simonides say, and according to you truly say, about justice?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: that the repayment of a debt is just, and in saying so he appears to me to be right.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: I should be sorry to doubt the word of such a wise and inspired man, but his meaning, though probably clear to you, is the reverse of clear to me. For he certainly does not mean, as we were just now saying, that I ought to return a deposit of arms or of anything else to one who asks for it when he is not in his right senses; and yet a deposit cannot be denied to be a debt.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then when the person who asks me is not in his right mind I am by no means to make the return?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly not.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: When Simonides said that the repayment of a debt was justice, he did not mean to include that case?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly not; for he thinks that a friend ought always to do good to a friend and never evil.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: You mean that the return of a deposit of gold which is to the injury of the receiver, if the two parties are friends, is not the repayment of a debt,—that is what you would imagine him to say?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And are enemies also to receive what we owe to them?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: To be sure they are to receive what we owe them, and an enemy, as I take it, owes to an enemy that which is due or proper to him—that is to say, evil.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Simonides, then, after the manner of poets, would seem to have spoken darkly of the nature of justice; for he really meant to say that justice is the giving to each man what is proper to him, and this he termed a debt.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: That must have been his meaning

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: By heaven! ; and if we asked him what due or proper thing is given by medicine, and to whom, what answer do you think that he would make to us?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: He would surely reply that medicine gives drugs and meat and drink to human bodies.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And what due or proper thing is given by cookery, and to what?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Seasoning to food.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And what is that which justice gives, and to whom?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: If, Socrates, we are to be guided at all by the analogy of the preceding instances, then justice is the art which gives good to friends and evil to enemies.

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is his meaning then?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: I think so.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And who is best able to do good to his friends and evil to his enemies in time of sickness?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: The physician.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Or when they are on a voyage, amid the perils of the sea?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: The pilot.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And in what sort of actions or with a view to what result is the just man most able to do harm to his enemy and good to his friend?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: In going to war against the one and in making alliances with the other.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: But when a man is well, my dear Polemarchus, there is no need of a physician?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And he who is not on a voyage has no need of a pilot?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then in time of peace justice will be of no use?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: I am very far from thinking so.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: You think that justice may be of use in peace as well as in war?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Like husbandry for the acquisition of corn?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Or like shoemaking for the acquisition of shoes,—that is what you mean?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And what similar use or power of acquisition has justice in time of peace?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: In contracts, Socrates, justice is of use.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And by contracts you mean partnerships?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: But is the just man or the skilful player a more useful and better partner at a game of draughts?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: The skilful player.

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And in the laying of bricks and stones is the just man a more useful or better partner than the builder?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Quite the reverse.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then in what sort of partnership is the just man a better partner than the harp-player, as in playing the harp the harp-player is certainly a better partner than the just man?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: In a money partnership.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes, Polemarchus, but surely not in the use of money; for you do not want a just man to be your counsellor in the purchase or sale of a horse; a man who is knowing about horses would be better for that, would he not?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And when you want to buy a ship, the shipwright or the pilot would be better?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then what is that joint use of silver or gold in which the just man is to be preferred?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: When you want a deposit to be kept safely.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: You mean when money is not wanted, but allowed to lie?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Precisely.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: That is to say, justice is useful when money is useless?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is the inference.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And when you want to keep a pruning-hook safe, then justice is useful to the individual and to the state; but when you want to use it, then the art of the vine-dresser?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And when you want to keep a shield or a lyre, and not to use them, you would say that justice is useful; but when you want to use them, then the art of the soldier or of the musician?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And so of all other things;—justice is useful when they are useless, and useless when they are useful?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: That is the inference.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then justice is not good for much. But let us consider this further point: Is not he who can best strike a blow in a boxing match or in any kind of fighting best able to ward off a blow?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And he who is most skilful in preventing or escaping from a disease is best able to create one?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And he is the best guard of a camp who is best able to steal a march upon the enemy?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then he who is a good keeper of anything is also a good thief?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That, I suppose, is to be inferred.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then if the just man is good at keeping money, he is good at stealing it.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is implied in the argument.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then after all the just man has turned out to be a thief. And this is a lesson which I suspect you must have learnt out of Homer; for he, speaking of Autolycus, the maternal grandfather of Odysseus, who is a favourite of his, affirms that

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: ‘He was excellent above all men in theft and perjury.’

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And so, you and Homer and Simonides are agreed that justice is an art of theft; to be practised however ‘for the good of friends and for the harm of enemies,’—that was what you were saying?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: No, certainly not that, though I do not now know what I did say; but I still stand by the latter words.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Well, there is another question: By friends and enemies do we mean those who are so really, or only in seeming? Surely a man may be expected to love those whom he thinks good, and to hate those whom he thinks evil.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes, but do not persons often err about good and evil: many who are not good seem to be so, and conversely?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: That is true.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then to them the good will be enemies and the evil will be their friends? True.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And in that case they will be right in doing good to the evil and evil to the good?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: But the good are just and would not do an injustice?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then according to your argument it is just to injure those who do no wrong?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Nay, Socrates; the doctrine is immoral.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then I suppose that we ought to do good to the just and harm to the unjust?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: I like that better.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: But see the consequence:—Many a man who is ignorant of human nature has friends who are bad friends, and in that case he ought to do harm to them; and he has good enemies whom he ought to benefit; but, if so, we shall be saying the very opposite of that which we affirmed to be the meaning of Simonides. Very true : and I think that we had better correct an error into which we seem to have fallen in the use of the words ‘friend’ and ‘enemy.’

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: What was the error, Polemarchus?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: We assumed that he is a friend who seems to be or who is thought good.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And how is the error to be corrected?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: We should rather say that he is a friend who is, as well as seems, good; and that he who seems only, and is not good, only seems to be and is not a friend; and of an enemy the same may be said.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: You would argue that the good are our friends and the bad our enemies?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And instead of saying simply as we did at first, that it is just to do good to our friends and harm to our enemies, we should further say: It is just to do good to our friends when they are good and harm to our enemies when they are evil?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes, that appears to me to be the truth.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: But ought the just to injure any one at all?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Undoubtedly he ought to injure those who are both wicked and his enemies.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: When horses are injured, are they improved or deteriorated?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: The latter.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Deteriorated, that is to say, in the good qualities of horses, not of dogs?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes, of horses.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And dogs are deteriorated in the good qualities of dogs, and not of horses?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Of course.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And will not men who are injured be deteriorated in that which is the proper virtue of man?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And that human virtue is justice?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: To be sure.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then men who are injured are of necessity made unjust?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is the result.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: But can the musician by his art make men unmusical?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly not.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Or the horseman by his art make them bad horsemen?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Impossible.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And can the just by justice make men unjust, or speaking generally, can the good by virtue make them bad?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Assuredly not.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Any more than heat can produce cold?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: It cannot.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Or drought moisture?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Clearly not.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Nor can the good harm any one?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Impossible.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: And the just is the good?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then to injure a friend or any one else is not the act of a just man, but of the opposite, who is the unjust?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: I think that what you say is quite true, Socrates.

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: Then if a man says that justice consists in the repayment of debts, and that good is the debt which a just man owes to his friends, and evil the debt which he owes to his enemies,—to say this is not wise; for it is not true, if, as has been clearly shown, the injuring of another can be in no case just. I agree with you

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then you and I are prepared to take up arms against any one who attributes such a saying to Simonides or Bias or Pittacus, or any other wise man or seer?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: I am quite ready to do battle at your side

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Shall I tell you whose I believe the saying to be?

turns.speaker: polemarchus
turns.raw_text: I believe that Periander or Perdiccas or Xerxes or Ismenias the Theban, or some other rich and mighty man, who had a great opinion of his own power, was the first to say that justice is ‘doing good to your friends and harm to your enemies.’

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes ; but if this definition of justice also breaks down, what other can be offered?

input.objective: Is justice helping friends and harming enemies?

metadata.notes: Polemarchus dialogue: inherited definition from Simonides
